metadata.title: ShawnOS.ai | GTM engineering, built in public
metadata.description: One monorepo. One operating system. Every skill, post, and campaign runs through the same codebase.

hero.subtitle: One monorepo. One operating system. Every skill, post, and campaign runs through the same codebase.

explore.title: Explore
explore.description: Audit the landscape. Find where the pipeline leaks, what content gaps exist, and where automation can replace manual work.

plan.title: Plan
plan.description: Map the build order. Define what ships this week, what compounds over time, and what gets cut.

build.title: Build
build.description: Ship real infrastructure. Code the pipelines, write the content, wire the automations. Every piece lives in the monorepo.

ship.title: Ship
ship.description: Push to production. Every commit deploys. Every post publishes. No staging purgatory - build, test, ship.

compound.title: Compound
compound.description: Let it stack. SEO compounds. Content libraries grow. Automations run while you sleep. The system gets stronger every day.

darkInterlude.title: one monorepo. everything ships from here.
darkInterlude.subtitle: three sites. AI agents. content pipelines. GTM automation. all version-controlled.

q1.question: What is GTM engineering?
q1.answer: GTM engineering is the practice of building go-to-market systems with code. Instead of clicking through CRMs and spreadsheets, you write pipelines, automate outbound, build enrichment workflows, and ship content systems - all version-controlled and composable.

q2.question: What is ShawnOS?
q2.answer: ShawnOS is a monorepo that runs my entire professional operating system.
q2.answerCont: Three websites, a progression engine, AI agents, content pipelines, and GTM tools - all in one codebase. It's an experiment in running your career like a software product.

q3.question: Why build in public?
q3.answer: Building in public turns your learning process into content, your commits into proof of work, and your failures into lessons others can learn from. It compounds - every post, every update, every shipped feature is a permanent artifact that builds trust over time.

q4.question: What stack does this run on?
q4.answer: TypeScript monorepo with Next.js (3 sites on Vercel), Python for scripting and content generation, Claude AI for the agent layer, and launchd crons for automation. Everything ships from one git repo.

q5.question: Can I use this approach for my own career?
q5.answer: Absolutely. The core idea - treat your career like a product, ship artifacts daily, automate what you can, and let everything compound - works for any discipline. You don't need this exact stack. You need the mindset of systematic, visible output.

q6.question: What's the progression engine?
q6.answer: A gamification layer that tracks real daily output - blog posts, code commits, shipped features - and converts them to XP. You level up through 11 tiers with evolving pixel art avatars. It's not decorative; it reflects actual work done.

metadata.title: The Arc
metadata.description: Plumber. SDR. GTM Engineer. The full arc behind shawnos.ai and the operating system.
metadata.ogDescription: Plumber. SDR. GTM Engineer. The full arc.

intro.line1: every GTM creator has the same about page.
intro.quote: "I was an SDR. then I found Clay. now I teach people how to use it."
intro.line3: except mine starts with copper pipe and a blowtorch.

layers.heading: the layers
layers.description: I didn't go from SDR to GTM engineer in a straight line. I went through three layers, and each one rewired how I think about building.

plumber.title: plumber
plumber.subtitle: layer 1
plumber.body: before tech, I was a plumber. not a metaphor. actual plumbing. diagnosing problems by listening to walls, tracing pipes nobody else wanted to touch, fixing systems under pressure with no documentation.
plumber.lesson: every system has a logic. follow the flow. if something breaks upstream, everything downstream fails. and nobody respects the fix until the water stops leaking.

sdr.title: SDR
sdr.subtitle: layer 2
sdr.body: 200+ cold emails a day. primary domains, no warmup, SalesLoft sequences, Salesforce activity logging by hand. building buying committees from scratch in accounts nobody wanted.
sdr.lesson: volume is a teacher. rejection is data. and the people who manually grind through outreach are the ones who actually understand what should be automated later.

gtmEngineer.title: GTM engineer
gtmEngineer.subtitle: layer 3
gtmEngineer.body: I stopped working inside the systems and started building them. Clay tables, HubSpot automation, Instantly sequences, web reveal workflows, enrichment pipelines. the same work I used to do by hand, now running on infrastructure I designed.
gtmEngineer.lesson: the best builders are the ones who did the job before they automated it. you can't engineer what you don't understand.

layers.conclusion: none of these define me. all of them sharpen what I ship.

thesis.heading: the thesis
thesis.p1: I don't prompt once and ship whatever comes back. I use plan mode before I write a single line. I use ask mode when something breaks. I run sub-agents for parallel work. I test, I break things, I read the error, I fix it, I ship it.
thesis.p2: AI doesn't do the work for you. it accelerates the work you already understand.
thesis.p3: every page on this site was iterated, not generated. every skill was debugged before it was documented. every campaign template was tested on real pipeline before it became a playbook.
thesis.p4: the repo has over 400 files. zero of them were one-shot prompts.
